title: probabilistic-alert-smoothing-eval-gate: add stochastic CI gate to reduce noisy prompt-regression alerts
preview: Motivation: CI has been producing a high volume of transient prompt-regression alerts for low-volume cohorts. These noisy failures increase on-call toil and delay prioritization of true regressions. This change introduces a probabilistic/stochastic gating layer that smooths alerts by estimating a posterior over the cohort regression rate and only opening an incident when the posterior crosses a configurable credibility threshold. Design: - New CI hook `prob-gate` computes a Beta posterior over passed/failed sample counts per cohort, with configurable prior (alpha/beta) and lookback window. - Gate emits three outcomes: allow-fail (treat as transient), gated-fail (record but do not page), and open-incident (page and block merge). - A lightweight failover path triggers a full evaluation run for cohorts that are near-threshold to reduce false positives. - Integrates with existing eval-harness reporters and the CI summary step; metrics emitted for telemetry (posterior_mean, posterior_ci_low, posterior_ci_high, samples_considered). Backwards compatibility: Default configuration preserves current deterministic threshold behavior (alpha=1,beta=1,credibility=0.99) so rollout is opt-in. Configuration is exposed via ci/eval-hooks.yml and via environment variables for Dedicated/Private runs. Implementation notes: - Core logic implemented in eval_harness/hooks/probabilistic_gate.py - CI integration and annotations in ci/hooks/prob-gate.yml - Unit tests in tests/test_probabilistic_gate.py and an integration test fixture using a small synthetic prompt cohort to validate smoothing behavior - Docs in docs/dev/eval-gates.md and example config in examples/ci/prob-gate-example.yaml Checklist: - [x] Core algorithm implemented - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] CI config and annotations - [x] Docs + example config - [x] Telemetry counters and dashboards (PR links included) Impact: This should reduce alert noise for low-sample cohorts while preserving sensitivity for real regressions. Teams can tune alpha/beta/credibility to match risk tolerance. See release notes for how to opt-in and suggested defaults for evaluation suites.

title: add difficulty-calibrator-and-heuristic-resampler-ci-hook
preview: Motivation: CI quality gates have been vulnerable to false positives/negatives when prompt sets shift subtly in difficulty (length, novelty vs. anchor set, generation entropy). This PR introduces a difficulty-calibrator component that scores prompts by a composite difficulty metric and a heuristic resampler used both during local eval runs and as a CI hook to detect distributional shifts. What changed: 1) eval_harness/difficulty/calibrator.py: new module that computes a composite difficulty score per prompt (token-length-normalized surprisal proxy from a lightweight LM scoring pass, embedding distance to anchored prompt centroids, and response-model entropy estimate). 2) eval_harness/ci/hooks/difficulty_gate.py: CI hook that compares the baseline difficulty distribution (stored as a short histogram blob in CI artifacts) to the current run using a K-S style test plus a shift-aware regression control. 3) eval_harness/samplers/heuristic_resampler.py: on-disk reweighting/resampling strategy to rebalance evaluation batches by difficulty quintiles and optionally prioritize historically unstable prompts. 4) tests/*: unit tests and small integration smoke tests. 5) docs/eval/difficulty_calibrator.md: design doc and recommended thresholds for CI. Design notes / how it works: - Composite difficulty score is a weighted sum (configurable) of:  a) normalized surprisal estimate (fast model-forward on a small ngram LM checkpoint)  b) semantic novelty (embedding cosine distance to nearest 50-anchor prompts)  c) response entropy proxy derived from prewired model-forward token logits for a short rollout. - The CI hook computes two signals: (A) distributional shift score (K-S on difficulty scalar) and (B) difficulty-conditioned regression delta: prompts are bucketed into quintiles and the primary quality metric (e.g., F1/ROUGE/semantic-sim) is compared per-bin to baseline; the hook fails only if either (1) the overall shift exceeds a tunable threshold AND the difficulty-conditioned per-bin regression exceeds per-bin thresholds OR (2) there is a significant regression concentrated in a bin with low sample count that cannot be explained by sampling variance. - The heuristic resampler reweights the eval queue to match a target difficulty distribution (default: baseline quintile proportions) and includes an 'attenuate-hotspots' option to avoid repeatedly exposing the same unstable prompts. Testing & CI: - Added deterministic-seed tests for calibrator computations and a small simulated-run integration test that exercises the gate with contrived baseline/current distributions. - CI initially failed on test_rebalance_sample due to non-determinism in the random seed used by the sampling smoke test; fixed by pinning RNG in the test and re-running. - Full pipeline (unit + smoke + lint) passed on rerun. Rollout plan: - Enabled as opt-in in CI for 1 week (flag: ci.enable_difficulty_gate=false by default). After observing false positive rate <1% on main branch historical windows and one week of canary on the release/* lanes, flip to opt-out. - Ops/Oncall runbook section added in docs describing how to interpret per-bin regressions and how to backfill baseline histograms. Backwards compatibility: default behavior is no-op unless baseline artifact present and ci flag enabled; no breaking changes to public APIs.

title: Longtail SLO meter and annotation adapter
preview: Motivation: We have seen sporadic, low-volume prompt-set failures that escape per-run thresholds but aggregate into customer-visible issues over time (long tail). This PR implements a Longtail SLO Meter that maintains a decay-weighted time series of failure signals and a companion Annotation Adapter to attach contextual evidence (examples, model variant, env) to flagged prompt-sets. The goal is to surface gradual SLO drift earlier and enable actionable handoff to triage workflows without increasing noisy CI failures. Design summary: 1) longtail_meter module: computes an exponentially decayed aggregate for each prompt-cohort keyed by prompt_set_id + model_variant, supports configurable half-life and bucket lengths; 2) annotation_adapter: lightweight pluggable interface (sync+async) that writes structured evidence to eval metadata and an optional annotations store; 3) ci-integration: a CI soft-fail mode that transitions from local eval failure -> sustained longtail deviation -> automatic Linear ticket creation with pre-filled evidence. Backwards compatibility: default behavior is opt-in via eval config. No changes to existing eval schema unless the annotation_adapter is enabled. Config knobs: decay_half_life (minutes/hours/days), min_sample_threshold, sustained_window_count, alert_threshold. Testing: unit tests for aggregator math, integration tests with synthetic prompt-sets, end-to-end smoke run against nightly eval harness. Rollout plan: gated behind feature flag and gradual enabling for internal teams before rolling to public CI gating. Related work: ENG-4287 (slo instrumentation), ENG-4301 (annotation store RFC).

title: Contextual weighted sample allocator and gradual restrictor for stable eval gating
preview:
Motivation: CI evals have been noisy for lower-volume prompt cohorts. Teams are getting frequent false positives when a small cohort flips. This PR implements a contextual weighted sample allocator that allocates probe budget across prompt sets using a combination of: (1) prompt-level importance (manually tagged or derived from production hit rates), (2) recent drift score (rolling 7-day), and (3) semantic novelty (via embedding distance).

Design: The allocator produces proportional sample quotas per cohort and emits a per-run sampling plan. A complementary GradualRestrictor replaces the previous immediate hard-blocking CI hook: instead of a binary fail, we compute a severity score and apply a staged response (soft-fail annotations, delayed blocking windows, and adaptive replays). This reduces churn while preserving safety for true regressions.

What changed (high level):
- New allocator: eval_harness/sampler/contextual_allocator.py (core logic, pluggable backends)
- Gradual restrictor: eval_harness/ci/gradual_restrictor.py (CI hook adapter)
- Scheduler integration: eval_harness/orchestrator/sampling_scheduler.py (wiring + metrics)
- Tests: tests/samplers/test_contextual_allocator.py, tests/ci/test_gradual_restrictor.py
- Docs: docs/evals/sampling.md (usage + config knobs)

Compatibility and config: The allocator is off by default. Config keys under eval.sampling.contextual_enabled=true to enable. Backwards-compatible: if not enabled the legacy uniform sampler is used. GradualRestrictor is configurable via eval.ci.restrictor.mode with options [off, soft, gradual, strict].

Test plan: unit tests added with deterministic seeds, synthetic drift traces, and an integration smoke test in CI that runs a small sampling plan. Benchmarked on staging with existing prompt suites — observed 41% reduction in transient CI failures for low-volume cohorts while maintaining high sensitivity for seeded regressions.

Checklist:
- [x] Unit tests
- [x] Integration smoke
- [x] Docs
- [x] Perf profiling (staging)
- [x] Feature flag gated and default-off in prod

Related: ENG-2261 (sampling strategy), ENG-2278 (ci-stability initiatives).

title: Introduce hierarchical fidelity oracle and non-blocking CI adapter
preview: Motivation: existing CI gates are brittle for prompt sets with heterogeneous lengths, multi-turn contexts and low-frequency anchors which lead to noisy false positives. This PR introduces a hierarchical fidelity oracle that fuses multiple evaluation signals (semantic similarity, role-aware turn consistency, token-level saliency, and anchor stability) across temporal cohorts and prompt granularity, and exposes a non-blocking CI adapter that surfaces soft-fail quality regressions with prioritized remediation suggestions. Core behaviors: 1) compute per-prompt multi-signal fidelity vectors, 2) aggregate per-cohort with recency-aware weighting and uncertainty calibration, 3) emit composite fidelity score and explainability hints (top contributing tokens and failing turns), 4) provide a non-blocking CI decision path which fails only on high-confidence regressions and otherwise annotates PRs with risk tiers and suggested rerun probes. This change contains: implementation of scorer and adapter, integration tests, docs and an example anchor manifest. Checklist: [x] implement hierarchical_fidelity scorer; [x] adapter for CI (non-blocking by default, configurable thresholds); [x] integration tests with synthetic perturbation reruns; [x] docs and example anchor prompt set; [x] Linear links (ENG-4523, ENG-6110) added to PR. Testing notes: local unit and integration tests added; synthetic rerun harness simulates paraphrase/negation/noise and verifies attenuation of false positives vs current thresholded gate; metrics show 22% reduction in spurious CI failures on internal smoke corpus. Rollout plan: gate is opt-in per-repo via a small YAML config in .eval/config.yaml, default mode is "annotate-only"; after two weeks of opt-in telemetry, global adoption can be proposed. Backwards compatibility: adapter is opt-in and does not change existing blocking gates. Follow-ups: add cross-model calibration (ENG-6110) and dashboarding to Console for fidelity deltas.

title: Introduce influence-matrix eval hook and rollback scorer
preview:
Motivation: We've had recurring incidents where a small prompt tweak in a PR produced outsized regressions for a narrow but critical slice of prompt-sets. Existing CI smokechecks flag macro regressions but provide little signal about which prompts, tokens, or candidate edits are most influential. This PR adds an 'influence matrix' eval hook that attributes per-prompt and per-perturbation influence on aggregate metrics, and a lightweight rollback scorer that suggests the smallest prompt change(s) that would return metrics to baseline.

What this does: Implements an eval hook that runs alongside existing regression checks. For each prompt-set it computes an influence score per prompt and per change vector using a randomized leave-k-out approximation (bounded sampling plus importance-weighting) and aggregates into an influence matrix (prompt x perturbation). The CI adapter consumes aggregated scores and will soft-block PRs that exceed a configurable impact threshold, surface a ranked list of top-k harmful prompts in the PR UI, and attach a rollback_scorer report with candidate minimal edits (text span revert or constraint relaxations) with estimated delta impact.

Design notes: The algorithm favors speed and interpretability over exact Shapley values; we approximate contribution via stratified sampling and an anchored baseline. Influence values are normalized per-metric and can be weighted by metric severity (e.g., hallucination > BLEU-like). The rollback scorer uses greedy local search over candidate revert spans and prompt parameter toggles (system message, temperature hints) to propose edits with confidence bands.

Backward compatibility: Hook is opt-in per-eval-job via job config and defaults to disabled for long-running production backfills. When enabled it adds a bounded extra eval cost (~10-30% depending on cohort size). The hook exposes tunables: sample_budget, top_k_reports, soft_block_threshold, metric_weights.

Files changed: See files_changed field for a concise list.

Related: ENG-2319 (detect high-impact prompt regressions ahead of rollout), PM-412 (UI work to display ranked prompt influence in PR).

title: Introduce contextual cohort synthesis gate with adaptive weighting
preview:
Motivation: We frequently see spurious regression signals when prompt distributions shift subtly (new metadata, truncated contexts, or injected system instructions). This PR introduces a CI/managed-gate that synthesizes cohorts at evaluation time based on semantic context clusters and adaptively reweights alert signals so short-term distribution shifts do not produce noisy failures.

What this PR does: 1) Adds a new gate type 'contextual_cohort_synthesis' that can be plugged into the CI eval pipeline; 2) Implements a lightweight vector-backed cohort builder that groups examples by a configurable semantic window (prompt+metadata embedding); 3) Computes weighted regression scores across synthesized cohorts with an attenuation factor tied to cohort volatility; 4) Exposes config knobs (min_cohort_size, volatility_smoothing, fallback_anchor) and a canary rollout policy; 5) Emits detailed telemetry (per-cohort counts, volatility, score_delta, attenuation_applied) to the eval metrics pipeline for downstream dashboards and alerting.

Checklist: - [x] Implementation with unit tests and integration smoke tests; - [x] CI hook integration and example workflow; - [x] Telemetry and dashboard dashboard-ready metrics names; - [x] Documentation snippet for ops and config; - [x] Backfill script to re-run recent failing prompt-sets through the gate.

Usage: The gate is opt-in via eval.yml: gates:
  - type: contextual_cohort_synthesis
    config:
      enabled: true
      min_cohort_size: 8
      volatility_smoothing: 0.6
      fallback_anchor: historical_baseline
      attenuation_threshold: 0.15

Testing notes: Unit tests cover the cohort builder, volatility calculation and attenuation rules. Integration tests run on a small synthetic prompt-set fixture (tests/fixtures/semantic-shift/*) and CI includes a smoke run against a clipped production sample. Local dev: ./tools/eval_runner --gate contextual_cohort_synthesis --sample tests/fixtures/semantic-shift/sample.json.

Risk & fallback: When enabled without an anchor, the attenuation can hide true regressions; default is disabled and CI templates include explicit opt-in. Canary rollout uses a small-sample flag and auto-fallback to the historical baseline gate when cohort volatility exceeds 0.9.

Related: ENG-4321 (contextual gating), PM-1189 (reduce false positive regression alerts), SUP-2210 (customer reported noisy CI alerts).

title: Add regression blame and diff-summary CI check for prompt-set evaluations
preview: Motivation: CI currently reports failing prompt-set metrics but gives limited context to quickly identify the source of a regression. This PR introduces a two-part CI hook: (1) regression-blame which correlates failing prompt cohorts to recent changes in prompt definitions, eval configs, and model pinning; (2) diff-summary which renders a compact human-readable summary of model-output changes for affected prompts. The goal is to reduce mean time-to-diagnose for eval regressions and to make gating decisions actionable. Implementation notes: regression-blame walks the prompt-set git history and the eval config commits in the last 30 days to produce candidate commit ranges; it then scores candidates by overlap with changed prompt IDs, changed tokenization metadata, and run-time seed/temperature differences. diff-summary runs a lightweight paired-output comparator that highlights token-level deltas, normalized scores, and a short semantic-change heuristic (BLEU/ROUGE delta + embedding cosine delta threshold). The CI hook plugs into existing eval GH actions and produces an artifact JSON and a Markdown summary comment. Checklist: [x] core algorithm and scoring, [x] GH action integration, [x] unit tests for scoring heuristics, [x] docs + runbook snippet, [x] small-scale perf bench on large prompt sets (< 10s per 100 prompts on CI runner). Related: Linear ENG-1882, ENG-2001. Jira: SUP-433 (customer report that prompted this work). Usage: when a gated eval fails, the CI will post a comment with a compact diff-summary and a ranked list of probable commits to inspect. The comment links to the full JSON artifact and a generated local html for quick triage. Notes for reviewers: the scoring weights are configurable via eval-config.yaml; defaults are conservative to avoid noisy blame; thresholds are documented. Rollout: implemented behind a feature flag eval_ci_blame enabled for 2 teams initially. This description contains implementation rationale, test plan, and rollout guidance in one continuous string.

Implementation notes: regression-blame walks the prompt-set git history and the eval config commits in the last 30 days to produce candidate commit ranges; it then scores candidates by overlap with changed prompt IDs, changed tokenization metadata, and run-time seed/temperature differences. diff-summary runs a lightweight paired-output comparator that highlights token-level deltas, normalized scores, and a short semantic-change heuristic (BLEU/ROUGE delta + embedding cosine delta threshold). The CI hook plugs into existing eval GH actions and produces an artifact JSON and a Markdown summary comment. Checklist: [x] core algorithm and scoring, [x] GH action integration, [x] unit tests for scoring heuristics, [x] docs + runbook snippet, [x] small-scale perf bench on large prompt sets (< 10s per 100 prompts on CI runner). Related: Linear ENG-1882, ENG-2001. Jira: SUP-433 (customer report that prompted this work). Usage: when a gated eval fails, the CI will post a comment with a compact diff-summary and a ranked list of probable commits to inspect. The comment links to the full JSON artifact and a generated local html for quick triage. Notes for reviewers: the scoring weights are configurable via eval-config.yaml; defaults are conservative to avoid noisy blame; thresholds are documented. Rollout: implemented behind a feature flag eval_ci_blame enabled for 2 teams initially. This description contains implementation rationale, test plan, and rollout guidance in one continuous string.

title: conditional eval fanout + weighted signal aggregator and CI gating
preview: Motivation: when a primary prompt-set check trips, we often need to run a set of targeted probes (e.g. hallucination prober, paraphrase-stability, token-saliency) to determine failure mode before blocking rollouts. This PR adds a conditional eval fanout layer and a weighted signal aggregator so CI can produce a single composite gating decision that reflects both severity and breadth of regressions. Design summary: - New runtime component eval_fanout orchestrates conditional parallel probes with a budget cap and backpressure to avoid blowing smoke-test windows. - WeightedSignalAggregator implements decay-weighted aggregation across cohorts and probes; weights are configurable per-linear-task and default to severity-first then coverage. - CiHook composite_slo_check integrates into existing eval CI: primary prompt-set runs as usual; on fail, fanout triggers configured probes; aggregator emits a human-readable breakdown and a single pass/fail decision. - Added JSON schema for fanout plan (configs/ : fanout-plans/*.json) and migration docs. Changes (high level): - eval/engine/fanout.py (core orchestration + retry/backoff) - eval/metrics/aggregator.py (aggregation logic, normalization) - eval/ci/composite_slo_hook.py (CI entrypoint + reporter) - configs/fanout-plans/english-and-multilingual.json - tests/{unit,integration}/test_fanout_* and smoke scenarios - docs/eval/fanout-design.md (motivation and configuration guide) Checklist: - [x] Unit tests covering core aggregator math and scheduling heuristics - [x] Integration smoke that runs fanout on a small sample prompt set within CI budget - [x] CI gating rule added and tested in staging - [x] Backwards-compatible configs (existing eval runs unchanged unless plan specified) Rollout plan: merge to main, enable composite_slo_check behind feature-flag per-repo, monitor eval/telemetry.composite_gate.* metrics for two weeks, then flip default for canaries. Related: ENG-2048 for production tuning, ENG-2110 for policy UI knobs. Notes: kept probes as light-weight callable adapters so adding new probes doesn't require changing orchestration core. See internal doc: https://linear.app/redwood/board/ENG-2048/fanout-spec for full design.

title: Lightweight quality spot-check hooks for perf suite runs
preview:
Summary: Perf regressions are easy to detect, but we’ve had a few near-misses where perf-focused changes accidentally altered decoding behavior (e.g., defaults drift, chat template mismatch, tool-call formatting toggled, or truncation due to max_tokens changes). This PR adds an optional, lightweight “quality spot-check” hook to run at the end of a perf run, sampling a small prompt set and applying basic sanity checks. It is not intended to be a full quality gate; it is intended to catch obvious breakages early and provide artifacts for triage.

Motivation / context: As part of the Q1 2026 “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” work, we’re standardizing perf runs and integrating canaries/gating. Applied ML asked for minimal guardrails so that we don’t ship a perf win that is actually a decoding/config regression. This PR implements the minimal spot-check plan aligned with the internal memo “perf-vs-quality-guardrails-q1-2026” and is designed to be safe to run in CI and on benchmark fleets.

What’s included: (1) A new optional spot-check phase: `eval-harness spotcheck` can be invoked standalone, or via the existing perf runner hook (`--quality-spotcheck`). The spot-check runs after perf metrics collection by default to avoid perturbing latency/throughput measurements. (2) Prompt set + sampling: Adds `prompts/spotcheck/v1/` with a small, synthetic + hand-curated “must-not-break” set (chat + completion + tool-call shaped prompts). Sampling is deterministic based on `--spotcheck-seed` (default ties to the perf run seed if present). No production prompt content is included. (3) Sanity checks (fast heuristics): non-empty output; UTF-8 decodes; no immediate server-side error indicators; maximum repetition guard (token-level repetition ratio threshold) to catch “stuck loop” regressions; finish_reason presence for supported backends; optional JSON parse check when the prompt declares `expect_json=true`; optional tool-call structure check when the prompt declares `expect_tool_call=true` (checks that the model returns a structured tool call payload vs plain text for our tool-call adapter).

Interfaces / CLI: New flags: `--quality-spotcheck` (bool), `--spotcheck-prompts prompts/spotcheck/v1/prompts.jsonl`, `--spotcheck-sample-size 20` (default 20), `--spotcheck-seed`, `--spotcheck-concurrency 2` (default 2), `--spotcheck-fail-mode warn|hard` (default warn), `--spotcheck-timeout-ms 60000`. Environment: `EVAL_HARNESS_SPOTCHECK=1` enables spot-check in environments where wiring flags is annoying (e.g., some perf-canary configs). Exit codes: `warn` mode always exits 0 but emits a summary and marks failing cases in the artifact; `hard` mode exits non-zero if any prompt fails sanity checks.

Artifacts: Spot-check writes a small JSONL artifact alongside the perf artifacts (for dashboard drill-down and to attach to run manifests). Each line is a single case with: prompt_id, mode (chat/completion), model, region (if present in env), seed, decoding_params hash, ok, checks_failed, finish_reason, output_len_chars, output_len_tokens (when tokenizer available), latency_ms, and a truncated output preview (first ~300 chars) for quick triage. Example snippet (illustrative): `{ "prompt_id": "tool_call_weather_003", "ok": false, "checks_failed": "tool_call_missing;finish_reason_missing", "latency_ms": 412, "output_preview": "Sure—here’s the weather..." }`.

Implementation details: Introduces `SpotcheckRunner` with a narrow interface so benchmark-lab/perf-canary can call it without pulling in perf measurement code paths. Sampling is stable: we shuffle prompt IDs with a seeded RNG and take the first N; prompts can be filtered by mode. Tokenization is optional (if the backend exposes tokenizer info we use it; otherwise repetition checks fall back to character-gram heuristics). All checks are designed to be O(n) over the output and cheap vs a real eval.

Why it won’t contaminate perf results: By default, spot-check runs after the perf workload completes. It uses a separate client instance with its own request IDs and emits metrics under a different namespace/tag set so dashboards can separate perf measurements vs spot-check runs. If you intentionally want to interleave spot-check with perf (not recommended), you can pass `--spotcheck-phase pre` or `--spotcheck-phase interleave` but both are explicitly marked experimental in docs.

Docs: Adds `docs/spotcheck.md` describing usage, typical failure modes, and how to use the artifacts in triage. Also documents the privacy stance (no prod prompt content; prompts are synthetic/handwritten) and intended scope (“sanity checks, not a quality score”).

Tests: Unit tests for prompt parsing/validation, deterministic sampling, JSON parse expectation, tool-call expectation, and repetition guard logic. Added a small integration test with a mocked backend to ensure warn vs hard exit behavior and artifact emission.

Commits (squash-merged): (1) add spotcheck runner + CLI plumbing, (2) add prompt set v1 + schema/validator, (3) add sanity checks and artifact writer, (4) docs + tests, (5) address review feedback (determinism + fail-mode semantics).

Files / areas changed (high level): `src/spotcheck/runner.py`, `src/spotcheck/checks.py`, `src/spotcheck/prompts.py`, `src/cli.py` (flag wiring), `prompts/spotcheck/v1/prompts.jsonl`, `prompts/spotcheck/v1/schema.json`, `docs/spotcheck.md`, `tests/test_spotcheck_*`.

Review notes / discussion highlights: Claire Dubois: requested that the sampling be explicitly tied to the run manifest seed to support reproducibility across reruns; updated so `--spotcheck-seed` defaults to `RUN_MANIFEST.seed` when present, with an explicit log line. Rina Kobayashi: concerned about spot-check affecting perf runs on shared benchmark fleets; moved default phase to post-perf and added a clear warning + tag separation. Dr. Maya Srinivasan: requested stricter language in docs about what this is and isn’t, and confirmation that prompts are non-sensitive; added privacy note and removed an earlier draft prompt that was too close to a real customer workflow. Mateo Alvarez / Sofia Ivanova: asked that hard failures be opt-in until we have baseline failure rates; set default fail-mode to warn and added guidance for when to flip to hard (release gating).

CI: Initially failed `lint-python` due to unused import in checks module; fixed in follow-up commit. Final checks green: unit tests, lint, typecheck, packaging.

Outcome: Merged (squash) into main. Follow-ups: After this lands, perf-canary will wire `--quality-spotcheck` for nightly runs in warn mode for a calibration period; we’ll propose moving release-branch gating to hard mode once we have a stable baseline failure rate and a small allowlist of known-benign flakes.

title: Introduce variant-weighted phased gate and explainable failure reporter
preview:
Motivation: We need a gate that can phase-in stricter regression checks across multiple prompt-set variants and weight them by production traffic mix. Existing gates are either binary or treat variants equally, which causes noisy failures from low-impact variants and slow rollouts when a single high-volume variant changes. This PR implements a phased "variant-weighted" quality gate plus an explainable failure reporter that surfaces per-variant contribution and suggested mitigations.

Summary of changes: added a PhaseGate orchestration that accepts variant weights and stage thresholds, a scoring adapter that produces per-variant metrics (accuracy, token-loss, semantic-distance), and an explainability module that generates a short failure report with example prompts, model outputs, and a concise mitigation recommendation. The gate supports three phases (shadow, canary, enforce) and automatically escalates or rolls back based on weighted aggregate score and high-impact-variant signals.

Why this is different: unlike our ensemble or cohort gates this approach natively models traffic-weighted importance, reduces alert fatigue from rare variants, and provides human-readable remediation suggestions to speed incident response. It also integrates with the CI decision buffer to allow staged rollouts during merge checks.

Checklist: - [x] PhaseGate core implementation - [x] Explainable failure reporter - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] CI hook to evaluate only affected prompt-sets - [x] Telemetry (per-variant metrics and overall weighted score) - [x] Documentation + runbook snippet

Backwards compatibility: The gate is opt-in; existing configuration remains unchanged. Defaults preserve current behavior by uniform weighting and single-phase enforcement.

Operational notes (included in runbook): - The report includes top-5 contributing prompts per-variant and a suggested temporary mitigator (shadow traffic split or pin previous model). - Recommended default weights are derived from a weekly traffic sampler and can be overridden per-linear ticket.

Release notes: add a new "variant-weighted phased quality gate" for eval-harness; provides explainable failure reports and phased rollouts to reduce noisy CI failures and speed triage.

title: Introduce per-prompt temporal baseline probe, drift-aware CI gate, and lightweight counterfactual replayer
preview:
Motivation: Some prompt-set regressions we observe are intermittent and tied to recent temporal shifts in training data or model weights. Existing cohort/anchor approaches flag regressions but often require manual baseline selection or suffer from noise when the underlying distribution evolves. This PR adds a Per-Prompt Temporal Baseline (PTB) probe that maintains short- and long-window baselines per prompt, a drift-aware CI gate that reasons about recent baseline movement before failing a job, and a lightweight counterfactual replayer used to prioritize regressions for human review.

Design summary: The PTB probe (eval_harness.probes.ptb_probe) computes exponentially weighted aggregates (EWMA) of configured metrics per prompt across two windows: short (default 1d) and long (default 30d). The probe exposes three signals: absolute delta (short vs long), variance ratio, and a stability score combining token-level variance and aggregated metric delta. The CI gate (ci/hooks/ptb_gate.py) uses these signals to decide fail/soft-fail behavior: if absolute delta exceeds the hard threshold we fail; if delta is between soft and hard thresholds we emit a soft-fail with attached diagnoses and optionally enqueue counterfactual replays.

Counterfactual replayer: A new lightweight replayer (eval_harness.tools.counterfactual_replayer) can replay a small, latency-bounded subset of sampled historical inputs against the PR head model variant and against a pinned stable model to produce a prioritized list of likely regressions. The replayer is intentionally cheap (bounded tokens and sample size) so it can run in CI without blowing budgets. It includes deterministic seeding options for reproducibility.

APIs and config: Introduces a new probe registration entry in probes/registry.yaml with tunable params (short_window, long_window, ewma_alpha, soft_threshold, hard_threshold, replay_budget). CI integration exposes these flags via .github/workflows/eval.yml as optional job inputs. Default gate behavior is non-blocking (soft-fail) for self-serve runs; for release branches the gate is enabled as hard-fail.

Testing and verification: Added unit tests covering EWMA convergence, signal thresholds, and replayer determinism (tests/test_ptb_probe.py). Added an integration smoke job in ci/integration/ptb_smoke.yml that runs the probe on a synthetic prompt set and validates gating behavior. Local benchmarks show the replayer adds ~8% CI runtime under the default replay_budget=50 samples.

Rollout plan: Start with opt-in on main via a feature flag (env PTB_GATE_MODE=soft). After two weeks of telemetry (false-positive rate, queue depth, latency) we plan to flip the default to hard for release branches only and surface auto-created prioritized tickets in Linear for prompts with persistent hard-fail signals.

Migration notes: No breaking changes to existing probe interfaces. Existing registries will pick up the PTB probe by default but the gate remains opt-in. See docs/eval/ptb.md for configuration and troubleshooting.

Related: Linear ENG-1984 (per-prompt stability monitoring), PM-422 (release gate automation). Support ticket SUP-6123 provided customer repro prompting this work.

title: Add trigger endpoint for Optimize quality gating (config diff + baseline)
preview: Context: Optimize needs a first-class way to run offline evals against a proposed serving config change (batching/caching/quantization profile) before allowing apply/rollout. This PR adds a new API endpoint and job plumbing in eval-harness to accept a config diff + baseline reference, execute the requested gate tier against selected prompt sets, and return structured metrics plus artifact pointers suitable for Console/Optimize consumption. Endpoint: POST /api/v1/gates/trigger (auth: service-to-service token; scoped to optimize-gating). Request (JSON): gate_tier ("smoke"|"full"), model_id, baseline (baseline_model_version + baseline_runtime_config_ref), proposed (runtime_config_diff, optional proposed_model_version), prompt_sets (ids), sample_count (optional; defaults per tier), run_metadata (route_id, account_id, region, commit_sha, idempotency_key). Validation: limits request payload size, rejects unknown diff keys, enforces that baseline ref is immutable (no "latest"), and checks prompt set ids exist and are enabled for gating. Execution: enqueues a GateRun job to the existing eval runner queue, pins model + runtime settings for the duration of the run, and writes an immutable run record with normalized inputs for audit/debug. Response: 202 Accepted with gate_run_id + status_url + estimated_duration_s; once complete, GET /api/v1/gates/runs/{id} returns status, summary metrics (per prompt set and overall), and artifact pointers (artifact_store_uri + optional signed_url when allowed), including: raw per-example outputs, rubric/score breakdowns, and a compact "comparison.json" (baseline vs proposed deltas). Artifacts: stored under artifact_store_uri prefix gates/{gate_run_id}/..., with retention aligned to existing eval artifact policy; Optimize can deep-link using gate_run_id. Observability: emits gate_trigger_requests_total and gate_run_enqueue_latency_ms (tags: gate_tier, model_id, region, caller=optimize) and attaches trace/span attributes for correlation from Optimize -> eval-harness. Tests: adds contract tests for request validation + schema stability, a fake runner integration test that asserts correct pinning of baseline/proposed configs, and golden JSON snapshots for the run detail response. Notes: This PR does not implement flake scoring/rerun aggregation (tracked in pr-418); response schema includes an optional flake_score field but it is not populated yet. Rollout: endpoint is behind feature flag OPTIMIZE_GATING_TRIGGER_API and requires allowlisted client identity; we can safely deploy without enabling any callers.
text: Context: Optimize needs a first-class way to run offline evals against a proposed serving config change (batching/caching/quantization profile) before allowing apply/rollout. This PR adds a new API endpoint and job plumbing in eval-harness to accept a config diff + baseline reference, execute the requested gate tier against selected prompt sets, and return structured metrics plus artifact pointers suitable for Console/Optimize consumption. Endpoint: POST /api/v1/gates/trigger (auth: service-to-service token; scoped to optimize-gating). Request (JSON): gate_tier ("smoke"|"full"), model_id, baseline (baseline_model_version + baseline_runtime_config_ref), proposed (runtime_config_diff, optional proposed_model_version), prompt_sets (ids), sample_count (optional; defaults per tier), run_metadata (route_id, account_id, region, commit_sha, idempotency_key). Validation: limits request payload size, rejects unknown diff keys, enforces that baseline ref is immutable (no "latest"), and checks prompt set ids exist and are enabled for gating. Execution: enqueues a GateRun job to the existing eval runner queue, pins model + runtime settings for the duration of the run, and writes an immutable run record with normalized inputs for audit/debug. Response: 202 Accepted with gate_run_id + status_url + estimated_duration_s; once complete, GET /api/v1/gates/runs/{id} returns status, summary metrics (per prompt set and overall), and artifact pointers (artifact_store_uri + optional signed_url when allowed), including: raw per-example outputs, rubric/score breakdowns, and a compact "comparison.json" (baseline vs proposed deltas). Artifacts: stored under artifact_store_uri prefix gates/{gate_run_id}/..., with retention aligned to existing eval artifact policy; Optimize can deep-link using gate_run_id. Observability: emits gate_trigger_requests_total and gate_run_enqueue_latency_ms (tags: gate_tier, model_id, region, caller=optimize) and attaches trace/span attributes for correlation from Optimize -> eval-harness. Tests: adds contract tests for request validation + schema stability, a fake runner integration test that asserts correct pinning of baseline/proposed configs, and golden JSON snapshots for the run detail response. Notes: This PR does not implement flake scoring/rerun aggregation (tracked in pr-418); response schema includes an optional flake_score field but it is not populated yet. Rollout: endpoint is behind feature flag OPTIMIZE_GATING_TRIGGER_API and requires allowlisted client identity; we can safely deploy without enabling any callers.

title: Add token-attribution prober and rollback-suggestion CI adapter
preview:
Motivation: We observed regressions that only manifest at the token level (e.g., a previously rare hallucinated sub-token pattern increasing frequency in certain prompt cohorts). Existing cohort-level SLOs catch change in aggregate metrics but not subtle token-attribution shifts. This PR introduces a lightweight token-attribution prober that runs as part of eval-harness smoke checks and a CI adapter that suggests minimally invasive rollback patches when attribution drift exceeds configured thresholds.

Summary: Adds a token-level attribution probe that integrates model attribution signals (attention-based saliency and logit-difference scoring) into the eval pipeline, produces per-prompt per-token drift heatmaps, and new CI logic that: 1) compares attribution fingerprints to historical anchors, 2) raises soft or hard failures depending on severity, and 3) emits rollback suggestions (prompt edits or anchor-replay patches) for triage. The description below details design, testing, and rollout.

What changed (high level):
- New module eval_harness/probers/token_attribution_prober.py: core probe implementation and pluggable attribution backends.
- New CI adapter eval_harness/ci/adapters/rollback_suggester_adapter.py: consumes probe output and converts detected regressions into actionable suggestions and GitHub-flavored comment payloads for PRs.
- Added a lightweight CLI hook bin/run-token-prober for local reproductions.
- Integrations: hooks registered in eval_harness/orchestrator/hooks.yaml and a new metrics exporter eval_harness/metrics/token_drift_metrics.py.
- Tests: unit and integration tests under tests/probers and tests/ci to validate detection logic and suggestion generation.
- Docs: docs/ci/token-attribution-prober.md with usage, thresholds, and recommended rollout knobs.

How it works (technical):
1) Attribution backends: the prober supports multiple attribution signals. By default we compute token saliency as delta in token logit probability when prefix tokens are ablated (fast approximation implemented) and augment with attention-weighted saliency when available from model traces. Backends are pluggable so teams can add model-specific explainers.
2) Fingerprint generation: for each prompt cohort we compute aggregated token-attribution histograms and normalize them into a fingerprint vector. We store epochal anchors (daily snapshots) and compute a symmetric KL + L2 composite distance to detect shifts.
3) CI gating: adapter compares current fingerprint to the pinned anchor for the target prompt set. Thresholds: soft (suggestion): distance > d_soft (defaults documented); hard (CI fail): distance > d_hard. Soft failures post a triage suggestion comment and attach a mini-report; hard failures block merge.
4) Rollback suggestions: when a soft or hard drift is detected we compute a minimal set of prompt edits or anchor-replay configurations (e.g., revert to prior canonical prompt variant or add an anchor replay entry) and include a suggested patch diff (applies to prompt-set YAMLs) that maintainers can accept with a single click. Suggestions are heuristic and flagged for human review.

Testing & validation:
- Unit tests for attribution math (100% branch coverage for the math kernels).
- Integration test that replays a seeded synthetic drift (see tests/probers/data/synthetic_drift.json) and asserts the adapter flags both soft and hard cases.
- End-to-end run included in CI that executes the prober in Docker with a small open model (weights mocked via deterministic scorer) to keep runtime <90s in smoke runs.

Performance & cost considerations:
- The prober has a fast mode (approximate logit delta with single-token ablations) used in CI smoke runs and a thorough mode (attention saliency + multi-token perturbations) used in nightly anchors. Default CI run uses fast mode to bound cost.

Backward compatibility and rollout plan:
- Default behavior is non-blocking (soft suggestions) for the first two weeks after merge; auto-blocking can be enabled via feature flag in orchestrator config.
- We ship a migration that backfills epochal anchors for the last 14 days; backfill job included at bins/backfill/token_anchor_backfill.py.

Operational signals and dashboards:
- Exposes Prometheus metrics: eval_token_drift_score_total, eval_token_drift_alerts{severity}, eval_token_probe_latency_seconds.
- Added Grafana dashboard JSON (dashboards/token-attribution.json) and an alert rule that triggers SRE paging only for sustained hard failures.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation code
- [x] Unit & integration tests
- [x] Docs and usage guide
- [x] CI integration and smoke entry
- [x] Rollout plan and backfill job

Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the default thresholds conservative to avoid noisy CI failures during ramp. Teams with sensitive prompt-sets (security/compliance) can opt-in to strict blocking via the orchestrator config.

Related work: This complements the token-saliency smokecheck introduced previously and ties into the cohort-stability framework (ENG-4921) to add a token-granular signal for regression triage.

Manual reproduction: See docs/ci/token-attribution-prober.md for step-by-step; quick reproduction: bin/run-token-prober --cohort tests/probers/data/example_cohort.yaml --mode fast

Merge notes: squash-merged after an extra CI run to ensure the nightly backfill job locks anchors properly.

title: Introduce proportional failure heatmap and context coverage CI hook
preview: Motivation: some regressions surface only in high-volume contexts or in narrow prompt cohorts; existing eval gates treat each failing example equally which underweights production impact. This PR introduces a proportional failure heatmap and a context-coverage metric that (1) weights failures by frequency and severity, (2) surfaces top-left hotspots in a time-decayed heatmap, and (3) produces a single coverage score used by the CI hook. Design: we cluster prompts into contextual cohorts using the existing prompt fingerprinting + semantic shingle embedding. For each cohort we compute a weighted failure score = sum_{examples in cohort}(failure_weight * recency_decay * frequency_factor). Frequency comes from the sampled traffic histogram (or sample proxy during CI). Severity is derived from configured eval metrics (exact-match, BLEU drop, hallucination flag), mapped to a 1-5 weight. We render a heatmap matrix: rows = cohorts, cols = failure types/buckets, cell = normalized proportional score. The context-coverage score is a normalized aggregate that penalizes under-tested or high-impact cohorts. CI behavior: runs as a post-eval hook. Default is soft-fail (annotate PR and open an advisory alert) and will fail the CI only when coverage < 0.6 AND proportional failure mass > threshold for two consecutive runs. There is an opt-in strict mode in repo eval.yaml to convert soft-fails into hard blocks. Implementation summary: - New hook entry point: eval_harness/hooks/proportional_failure_heatmap.py - Cohort sampler & cache adaptor: eval_harness/samplers/contextual_cohort_sampler.py - Heatmap renderer + export: eval_harness/reporting/coverage_heatmap_v1.py - CLI/backfill helper: tools/heatmap_backfill.py - Dashboard defs: infra/dashboards/coverage-heatmap.json - Unit + integration tests: tests/hooks/test_proportional_heatmap.py + tests/samplers/test_contextual_cohort.py Backwards compatibility: hook uses existing eval outputs; no changes required to model outputs or existing prompt sets. Rollout plan: 1) Merge in soft-fail mode, enable on canary branch for 48 hours (automated job), 2) run backfill for last 30 days using tools/heatmap_backfill.py to populate dashboards, 3) after observing false positive rate < 4% and stable signal for 1 week, flip opt-in projects to strict gating. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests - [x] Integration test (CI job) - [x] Dashboard JSON added - [x] Documentation snippet added to docs/hooks.md - [x] Linear tickets linked (ENG-2345) - [x] Backfill tool included

title: Implement prompt fingerprint ledger and replay-safe CI enforcer
preview:
Motivation: Flaky eval failures at the prompt-set level continue to surface from subtle dataset composition drift (sampling, paraphrase swaps, and tokenization edge cases) rather than model regressions. This PR implements a compact on-disk ledger and a CI-side enforcer that (1) records stable fingerprints for prompt-sets+seed+model-config, (2) compares new runs against the ledger to flag composition-driven variance, and (3) provides a replay-safe enforcement mode that permits human review and controlled backfills before a hard CI failure.

Design summary: The fingerprint is a deterministic, salted hash that combines prompt identifiers, normalized prompt text, canonical tokenization signature, run-seed, and a lightweight output sketch (sampled token-level checksums plus aggregated metrics). A new ledger store (local by default; support for S3/GCS) holds one canonical entry per evaluated prompt-set+baseline ID. The CI enforcer (eval_ci_enforcer.py) is a thin adapter that runs after eval jobs complete: it computes fingerprints for the run, diffs against the ledger, and decides pass/warn/fail according to policy. Policies are configurable via the existing eval configuration (eval_config.yaml) with three modes: off, warn-only, and replay-safe-enforce. Replay-safe-enforce: when mismatches are detected, the job is marked 'blocked' and an automated backfill job is queued (if configured) or a human review workflow is created in Linear.

What changed (high level): added ledger implementation and on-disk format, CLI helpers to snapshot and backfill prompt-sets, CI enforcer adapter, configuration knobs, metrics and tracing spans, unit and integration tests, and PR-level documentation. No changes to existing eval metric implementations. The ledger schema is intentionally conservative to remain forward-compatible.

Checklist:
- [x] Fingerprint algorithm implementation (stable hashing + normalization)
- [x] Ledger write/read with S3/GCS/local adapters
- [x] CI adapter that integrates into our GitHub Actions eval workflow
- [x] Opt-in replay-safe-enforce policy and Linear backfill creation
- [x] Unit tests for fingerprinting and ledger operations
- [x] Integration test with a small synthetic prompt-set to validate end-to-end blocking/backfill path
- [x] Documentation section and examples in docs/eval/ledger.md

Compatibility & rollout: Default is off. We will enable warn-only for the old smoke-eval workflow for two weeks to collect telemetry, then enable replay-safe-enforce for new eval runs on the staging pipeline. The ledger migration is read-on-write; existing evals are not retroactively fingerprinted unless a backfill is triggered.

Performance: Fingerprinting adds ~15-40ms per prompt at 1k prompts (I/O dominated) in local mode and ~60-150ms when writing to remote object storage; these are small compared to token-generation time in typical LLM evals. The ledger size is compact (fingerprint + metadata ≈ 200B per prompt-set entry).

Operational notes: metrics emitted under eval.fingerprint.* (counts, mismatch_rate, backfill_queued) and tracing spans added to eval-run traces. The CI adapter annotates PRs with a succinct mismatch summary and a link to the ledger diff viewer.

Rollout plan: staged enablement (warn-only -> replay-safe-enforce opt-in -> org-wide enforcement) and a one-week monitoring window after each step. If mismatch_rate spikes from baseline, automatically open a Linear ticket assigned to the owning team for triage.

title: Introduce anchor-driven failure replay with progressive quarantine throttler
preview: Motivation: Spikes of noisy eval failures (often transient or infra-related) are triggering rollbacks and incorrect regression signals. This PR introduces an anchor-driven failure replay system that replays a small, provenance-anchored sample of failing requests against the candidate and baseline in isolation, plus a progressive quarantine throttler that limits the blast radius of cohorts that produce repeated non-deterministic failures. Key goals: reduce noisy CI failures, improve root-cause signal for real regressions, and keep reruns bounded and auditable. What this changes: 1) eval_harness/orchestrator/anchor_replay.py: new replay engine that captures request/response provenance and replays deterministically with KV-cache seeding; 2) ci/hooks/anchor_replay_hook.py: CI hook that marks failing eval jobs for anchored replay and triggers the replay job; 3) ci/configs/progressive_quarantine.yaml: tunable policy for tiered quarantine (soft hold -> partial throttle -> full quarantine) with exponential backoff and decay; 4) metrics: adds eval.anchor_replay.* tags, replay_outcome, quarantine_state and quarantine_duration to perf telemetry; 5) tests: integration and unit tests covering deterministic replay paths and quarantine transitions under simulated flakiness; 6) docs/ci/anchor-replay.md: operational runbook and troubleshooting. Backwards compatibility: the hook is opt-in by default for projects; existing eval pipelines are unchanged until the new ci job is enabled. Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked (ENG-1987) - [x] Unit + integration tests added - [x] Metrics and traces emitted - [x] Opt-in config and sane defaults - [x] Docs and runbook included How it works (brief): when an eval job fails, the hook attaches a compact anchor snapshot (seed prompt IDs + minimal context + tokenization fingerprints). The anchor-replay job takes those anchors, rehydrates deterministic inputs (fixing RNG seeds, KV cache priming) and runs the candidate and baseline in an isolated environment with strict timeouts and dual-run tracing. If a replay confirms a regression we escalate; if a replay is unstable (non-deterministic) we transition the cohort through the quarantine throttler which progressively reduces traffic routed to the cohort for live shadowing and increases sampling frequency for targeted repro attempts. Observability and safety: replay runs are tagged and stored, and the runbook documents triage steps to avoid accidental rollouts based solely on single-run failures. Performance considerations: replay is intentionally small (anchors limited by size and capped to N per job) to keep cost low; quarantine throttler uses exponential backoff to avoid constant reruns. Related work: builds on ideas in ENG-2042 (anchored probes) and the regression-blame tooling (prior PR).

title: Introduce contextualized minibatch regression watchdog and suppression adapter
preview:
Motivation: Flaky per-prompt regressions are noisy in CI when single minibatch failures trigger rollbacks or high-severity alerts. This PR adds a contextualized minibatch regression watchdog that computes short-horizon, context-aware regression signals and a suppression adapter that dampens alerts from transient minibatch noise. The goal is to reduce false positives in CI gating while preserving sensitivity to sustained quality regressions.

What this PR introduces: 1) src/watchdogs/contextual_minibatch_watchdog.py - new watchdog which calculates per-prompt-context rolling statistics (short window mean, variance, exponential smoothing) and emits a scored regression signal. 2) src/adapters/suppression_adapter.py - a pluggable adapter used in CI and alerting pipelines to apply hysteresis and suppression rules: confirmation windows, adaptive thresholds based on context prevalence, and a backoff multiplier for high-variance prompts. 3) src/metrics/contextual_variance_aggregator.py - a lightweight aggregator that computes cross-seed/context variance and integrates with existing EvalMetric interface. 4) ci/hooks/watchdog-ci.yml - CI hook that runs a focused smoke eval using minibatch shards and gates on sustained signal (configured as 3 consecutive windows). 5) tests/{unit,integration}/watchdog_* - unit and integration tests covering edge cases (all-zero tokens, variable batch sizes, missing contexts), plus a flakiness simulator for synthetic load.

Design notes: - The watchdog uses context keys derived from prompt metadata (domain, prompt_type, client_tier) so statistics are computed in semantically meaningful buckets. Bucketing is configurable at runtime. - Suppression adapter policy: require confirmation over N windows or N distinct shards before escalating. N defaults to 3 but is policy-driven per repo/route. - CI hook semantics: the CI job will fail only if the suppression adapter permits escalation. The default CI configuration is conservative (designed to reduce developer churn) but project leads can opt-in to stricter thresholds.

text:
Motivation: Flaky per-prompt regressions are noisy in CI when single minibatch failures trigger rollbacks or high-severity alerts. This PR adds a contextualized minibatch regression watchdog that computes short-horizon, context-aware regression signals and a suppression adapter that dampens alerts from transient minibatch noise. The goal is to reduce false positives in CI gating while preserving sensitivity to sustained quality regressions.

What this PR introduces: 1) src/watchdogs/contextual_minibatch_watchdog.py - new watchdog which calculates per-prompt-context rolling statistics (short window mean, variance, exponential smoothing) and emits a scored regression signal. 2) src/adapters/suppression_adapter.py - a pluggable adapter used in CI and alerting pipelines to apply hysteresis and suppression rules: confirmation windows, adaptive thresholds based on context prevalence, and a backoff multiplier for high-variance prompts. 3) src/metrics/contextual_variance_aggregator.py - a lightweight aggregator that computes cross-seed/context variance and integrates with existing EvalMetric interface. 4) ci/hooks/watchdog-ci.yml - CI hook that runs a focused smoke eval using minibatch shards and gates on sustained signal (configured as 3 consecutive windows). 5) tests/{unit,integration}/watchdog_* - unit and integration tests covering edge cases (all-zero tokens, variable batch sizes, missing contexts), plus a flakiness simulator for synthetic load.

Design notes: - The watchdog uses context keys derived from prompt metadata (domain, prompt_type, client_tier) so statistics are computed in semantically meaningful buckets. Bucketing is configurable at runtime. - Suppression adapter policy: require confirmation over N windows or N distinct shards before escalating. N defaults to 3 but is policy-driven per repo/route. - CI hook semantics: the CI job will fail only if the suppression adapter permits escalation. The default CI configuration is conservative (designed to reduce developer churn) but project leads can opt-in to stricter thresholds.

Compatibility and rollout: - Backwards compatible: new modules expose adapter hooks but do not change existing evaluation behavior unless enabled via config. - Rolling deployment plan: (a) land code behind feature flag, (b) enable on small set of noisy prompt-sets for 2 weeks, (c) expand to all smoke CI jobs if no missed regressions detected.

Notes: This PR focuses on detection and suppression for minibatch-level noise patterns. It intentionally avoids hard-coded model-level thresholds and instead exposes configuration knobs for per-team policy tuning.

title: Add ensemble-score-aggregator and CI regression hook for composite eval metrics
preview: Motivation: teams running multi-metric evals need a single, auditable signal for CI gating that reflects weighted regressions across prompt sets and metric families. This PR introduces an "ensemble score" aggregator that computes a weighted composite from a configurable set of metrics (e.g. BLEU, ROUGE-L, exact-match, embedding-sim) and a CI hook that fails builds when the ensemble score or any high-priority submetric regresses past configured thresholds. What changed: added aggregator implementation, config schema, CI integration, unit + integration tests, docs and migration notes. title: introduce-historical-variance-detector-and-variance-gate-hook
preview:
Motivation: We've seen intermittent metric oscillations across repeated eval runs (same prompt set, different seeds / sampling windows) that cause nondeterministic gate failures and noisy postdeploy regressions. This PR introduces a Historical Variance Detector (HVD) to detect when a prompt-set or cohort exhibits unstable score distributions over a sliding window of previous eval runs and optionally block merges.

What I changed (high level):
- Add a new module eval_harness/variance_detector.py that computes per-prompt and cohort-level variance, runs a two-sample KS test against the historical distribution, and produces a compact variance report.
- Add an opt-in GH Action workflow .github/workflows/variance-gate.yml that invokes the harness check with the new --variance-check flag on PRs targeting main and release/*.
- Extend the CLI: `harness check --suite <suite> --variance-check --lookback 8 --alpha 0.01 --delta-threshold 0.12` (defaults chosen to be conservative). The CLI returns non-zero if any cohort/prompt exceeds thresholds.
- Integrate an experimental `rollback_scorer` that surfaces the top-N prompts/cohorts contributing to variance; this hooks into the existing rollout recommendations UI (backend only) so SRE/Platform can inspect before rolling forward.
- Tests: add unit tests for distribution comparisons and an integration test that runs against a synthetic history store. Also add a small compatibility shim for older historical-run artifacts.

Commits (summary):
- c1f8b2e: initial variance-detector implementation + docs
- 7a2d9f4: add KS test and sliding-window logic; expose lookback/alpha flags
- e3b4a01: GH Action workflow for variance gate + small CI adapter
- 9f0a5c7: integrate rollback_scorer and add API surface for console
- f1d3b6a: tests and flaky-seed fix (see review notes)

Files changed (high level):
- eval_harness/variance_detector.py (+320 additions)
- eval_harness/cli.py (+18 additions) — new flags and wiring
- integration_tests/test_variance_gate.py (+42 additions)
- .github/workflows/variance-gate.yml (+45 additions)
- docs/variance.md (+30 additions)
- eval_harness/history/shim_compat.py (+20 additions)
- eval_harness/rollback_scorer.py (+35 additions)

Design notes and rationale: HVD is deliberately conservative: it only gates when both the normalized variance and a KS-test p-value indicate distributional shift beyond the delta threshold. This reduces false positives from isolated outliers. The sliding window defaults to 8 most recent eval runs (empirically chosen from internal history), but is configurable per-suite. We log compact histograms and top-contributors to make triage actionable.

Review conversation (abridged, chronological):
- Ethan Cole: "Can the detector be made resilient to differing sample sizes? If historical runs have different numbers of completions we should normalize or bootstrap." PRIYA: "Implemented sample-size-aware bootstrapped CIs in the latest commit; added integration test to cover uneven sizes." (commit 7a2d9f4)
- Maya Chen: "Please parameterize lookback and alpha at the suite level in addition to CLI flags; also document expected run cadence." PRIYA: "Added suite-level defaults in suite_config and updated docs/variance.md with recommended cadences." (commit e3b4a01)
- Luca Bianchi: "Flaky integration test: failing intermittently on CI due to RNG. Can we seed or use deterministic sampling?" PRIYA: "Fixed by seeding the synthetic generator and switching from random.gauss to numpy's Generator with PCG64 for deterministic draws in tests." (commit f1d3b6a)
- Maya Chen: "Approving — please squash on merge; would like a follow-up to wire alerts into Slack ops channel." (approval)
- Ethan Cole: "LGTM after one nit about log verbosity; not blocking." (approval)

CI & gating behavior observed on this PR:
- First run: variance-gate workflow failed due to the flaky integration test (intermittent rng). See job variance-integration (failed).
- Fix applied (seed deterministic RNG) and tests updated; second run: all checks passed (unit, integration, lint).
- ci_status: pass

Edge cases & limitations called out in review: historical store retention matters — if history is sparse the detector will gracefully degrade to a per-run warning mode rather than hard-failure. The KS test assumes continuous distributions; for very small sample sizes the detector falls back to a median-delta check (documented in docs/variance.md).

Compatibility & rollout plan: The variance gate is opt-in by default. We enabled it for the eval-harness repo in team CI to evaluate noise levels for 2 weeks. After that, teams can opt their suites into enforce=true to block merges automatically. The rollback_scorer is experimental and surfaced only as a backend API for now; Console integration will follow as a separate PR (ENG-4302).

Merge: Squash merge after approvals. Merged at 2025-11-05. Follow-ups: ENG-4302 (console UI), ENG-4311 (Slack alerts), and a cross-repo checklist to enable enforcement for prioritized suites.

Notes for QA/Oncall: If adopters see increased gate failures, check eval-harness history retention and per-suite lookback config. For urgent rollbacks, use the rollback_scorer API to identify candidate prompts to revert or to create targeted prompt filters.

title: Tag eval runs with model version + model-metadata schema hash
preview: Context: As part of the Model Metadata Schema v1 + automated changelog work, we need to be able to trace a customer-visible release note entry back to concrete eval evidence (and vice versa). Today the eval harness records the model identifier and some run params, but does not persist (a) the promoted model version string used for serving (e.g., catalog version/variant) and (b) the model-metadata schema hash that the changelog generator references. That makes it hard to answer support questions like “which exact model version did this eval run validate?” and blocks automated linking in release notes. What this PR does: 1) Adds required fields to the eval run manifest/results payload: model_version (string) and model_metadata_schema_hash (sha256 hex). 2) Populates these fields from the run config when available, and falls back to resolving via the model registry client (best-effort) when the caller provides model_id but not a version. 3) Propagates the tags to: run.json, summary.json, and the top-level artifact index so downstream tooling (changelog generator / Console) can join on (model_version, schema_hash) without parsing logs. 4) Adds CLI flags + env var support: --model-version, --model-metadata-schema-hash, with REDWOOD_MODEL_VERSION / REDWOOD_MODEL_METADATA_SCHEMA_HASH overrides (CI-friendly). 5) Adds validation: schema hash must be 64-char hex; model_version must be non-empty and match our semver-ish format (allows catalog suffixes like "+q4_k_m"). Compatibility: Existing stored runs remain readable; the reader treats missing fields as "unknown" and emits a warning (no hard failure). New runs will include the fields. Testing: unit tests for validation + serialization; integration test that runs a tiny harness workflow and asserts artifacts contain tags; golden-file update for run manifest. Rollout notes: we should update the CI templates in redwood-model-registry and the internal eval job definitions to pass the schema hash (follow-up in ENG-9827 checklist).

title: Introduce contextual ballot evaluator and safety-snapshot regression gate
preview:
Motivation: CI-driven functional regressions occasionally pass simple metric thresholds but still cause low-probability failure modes (safety, hallucination) to regress. This PR adds two complementary CI primitives: a Contextual Ballot Evaluator (CBE) that runs configurable re-samples of model responses and decides pass/fail by consensus ballots weighted by semantic similarity and prompt cohort importance; and a Safety Snapshot Gate that captures classifier score distributions at merge time and raises soft-fail alerts when tail mass shifts beyond tolerance.

What this change does: Adds a ballot aggregation engine with pluggable weighting (semantic similarity, precedent weight, and token-length normalization), a CI hook that executes N reruns in parallel with staggered seeds and computes vote distributions, and a safety snapshot artifact that is stored alongside CI artifacts for historical comparison. The gate emits structured signals consumed by the eval orchestrator to trigger soft quarantine or canary-focused rollouts.

Implementation notes: The ballot evaluator is implemented in eval_harness/core/ballot_evaluator.py with deterministic pseudo-random seeding and a replayable audit log. The CI hook lives in eval_harness/hooks/ci_ballot_hook.py and integrates with existing smoke runner. Safety snapshots are compact JSON braindumps capturing classifier score quantiles and cohort-level anchors and are emitted by eval_harness/hooks/safety_snapshot.py. Unit and integration tests added under tests/ to validate aggregation logic and snapshot diffing.

Checklist:
- [x] Ballot evaluator core logic and weighting strategies implemented
- [x] CI hook and pipeline integration added (ci/pipeline.yaml changes)
- [x] Safety snapshot emitter and historical comparator implemented
- [x] Tests added: unit, integration, snapshot regression simulation
- [x] Docs: docs/eval-playbooks/ballot_and_safety.md with operational guidance

Related work: builds on prior CI gating work (ENG-7203) and the contextual anchor patterns in ENG-7321. Adds an alternative to strict-threshold gates by providing probabilistic consensus and explicit snapshot-based drift detection.

Commit summary: Implement ballot aggregator and CI hook; Add safety snapshot writer and comparator; Add tests and mock fixtures; Update CI pipeline to run ballot hook on slow smoke; Add playbook docs with example linear job and runbook.

Merge decision: After two rounds of review and a CI rerun for flaky test coverage, reviewers approved and the PR was squashed and merged.

title: Introduce staggered probing schedule and failure-decay gate
preview:
Summary: Adds a time-staggered probing scheduler and a failure-decay gate to the eval CI pipeline. The staggered prober spreads probe execution across configurable windows to avoid thundering on sensitive model endpoints and to produce smoother rolling signals. The failure-decay gate introduces an exponentially decaying penalty for isolated failures so that transient noise does not immediately block rollouts.
Motivation: We observed several incidents where atomic spikes in flakiness (network blips, transient infra GC, tokenization flakiness) caused immediate hard failures in the regression gate, triggering noisy rollbacks. This change reduces false-positive blocks while preserving sensitivity to sustained regressions.
Design: - StaggeredProber: new scheduler that accepts a probe set and a window configuration (slot_count, jitter_seed, priority_buckets). It assigns probes to slots deterministically by shard key (prompt set id) with optional random jitter to smooth correlations. - FailureDecayGate: tracks per-probe failure scores and computes a smoothed failure rate using exponential decay (alpha-configurable). Gate fails only when the decayed failure rate exceeds threshold for N consecutive evaluation windows. - Integration: The prober plugs into the existing eval orchestrator as an alternate fanout mode; the gate is evaluated as part of the cluster of CI hooks after aggregated metrics step.
What changed (high level): - Added evaluator/scheduler/staggered_prober.py (core scheduling logic). - Added hooks/gates/failure_decay_gate.py (gate implementation and configuration). - Augmented orchestration: eval_orchestrator now supports --probe-mode staggered and plumbs a per-run window id. - New config: ci_defaults.yml has staggered defaults and decay params. - Tests: unit tests for scheduling determinism, integration test that simulates transient failures and verifies gate behaviour.
Rollout/flags: Default is disabled. Feature flag eval.staggered_prober enabled for canary fleets. FailureDecayGate enabled behind eval.failure_decay boolean; we will turn on for shadow runs for 72 hours before gating mainline merges.
Testing performed: - Local unit tests + mocked clock deterministic tests (added). - End-to-end integration in staging with a synthetic noisy backend reproduced the old false-positive pattern; the new gate retained sensitivity to sustained regressions while ignoring isolated spikes. - Load test: staggered mode reduced peak concurrent probe workers by ~68% under our synthetic sharded workload.
Observability and telemetry: - New metrics: eval.staggered.slots_utilization, eval.staggered.skew_conflict_rate, eval.failure_decay.decayed_rate, eval.failure_decay.window_alarms. - Added dashboard panels and SLO alert tweaks in observability-pack to avoid duplicate alerts during initial rollout.
Backwards compatibility: Existing probe modes (batch, immediate) unchanged. Gate is additive and can be toggled per-project.
Linear/Jira: Implements ENG-3421 (reduce noisy regression blocks) and ENG-3570 (probe scheduling improvements); related to SUP-8891 (customer-reported flakiness).
Risks and mitigations: - Misconfiguration could delay detection of true regressions: mitigated by conservative default thresholds and gradual rollout. - Scheduler determinism across workers: covered by deterministic sharding tests and cross-checked with end-to-end runs.
Checklist: - [x] Unit tests added - [x] Integration test added - [x] Docs updated (ci/README section) - [x] Feature flags wired - [x] Telemetry emitted
Release notes: "Introduce staggered probing schedule to reduce probe thundering and a failure-decay gating mechanism to reduce false-positive CI blocks. Disabled by default; enable via feature flags for canaries."

title: Add anchorwise fidelity audit and CI explainability hook
preview:
Motivation: Teams running long-lived prompt sets have asked for more granular, explainable regression signals tied to 'anchors' (representative examples) so that CI failures are actionable without manual triage. This PR introduces an anchorwise fidelity audit that computes per-anchor fidelity and saliency deltas, surfaces interpretable failure reasons (e.g., token shift, hallucinated span, idempotency drop), and wires a CI hook that annotates PRs with a compact explainability report.

What this changes: adds a lightweight audit runner (hooks/anchorwise_audit.py) that: (1) replays the prompt-set against a reference baseline and the candidate change, (2) computes per-anchor metrics (fidelity_score, token_saliency_delta, semantic_overlap_pct), (3) runs a stacked heuristic classifier to attribute probable failure cause, and (4) emits a normalized JSON+CSV summary consumed by the CI hook. The CI hook (ci/hooks/anchor_explainability_gate.yml) runs in the eval pipeline and will soft-fail on degradations exceeding configured thresholds and post an explainability comment to the PR with the top 5 anchors requiring attention.

Checklist (implementation & rollout):
- [x] Core audit runner implementation with unit tests
- [x] CI hook definition and comment poster integration
- [x] Documentation: docs/anchorwise_fidelity.md with metric definitions and remediation guidance
- [x] Backwards-compatible config: default thresholds set to conservative values and overridable via .evalrc in repo
- [x] Smoke run against recent canary commits to validate signal and false-positive rate
- [x] Telemetry hooks for false-positive collection and opt-out flag

Testing & validation: ran unit tests locally and in CI; performed a 48-hour shadow run on the redwood/main prompt set (shadow run id: shadow-20250318-02) to confirm the anchor ranking aligns with human triage in >85% of sampled cases. Thresholds were tuned to target ~5% soft-fail rate on historical diffs.

Rollout plan: merge to main with feature flagged disabled by default. Flip feature flag for eval-maintainers and run as hard gate for a single team for two weeks before organization-wide opt-in.

Risks and mitigations: heuristics can be noisy for very small anchors (<= 3 tokens); we filter those out and include a warning in the audit output. The CI hook is soft-fail by default to avoid blocking regressions in flight.

title: Tag eval outputs with experiment variant identifiers + publish compact score stream for Console
preview:
Motivation: Route-level A/B testing in Console needs a quality signal per {route, experiment_id, variant_id}. Today, eval-harness produces per-run artifacts (full JSON + HTML reports) but those are not consistently attributable to a routing experiment variant, and they’re too heavy for Console ingestion. This PR (1) propagates experiment attribution fields into eval execution and output artifacts, and (2) publishes a compact score stream suitable for near-real-time aggregation and dashboarding.

What’s included: (A) Variant tagging: add optional experiment context to eval runs, including experiment_id, variant_id, route_id, and a stable stickiness_key_hash (when provided), and persist these tags into (i) run metadata.json, (ii) per-prompt result rows, and (iii) summary.json. (B) Compact score stream: emit one record per {run_id, prompt_set_id, scorer_name} with normalized score, raw score, and minimal dimensions. This is designed to be ingested by Telemetry/Analytics and joined with experiment guardrails results without pulling full eval artifacts.

Schema (score stream v1): Each record is JSONL with fields: event_type=eval_score_v1, emitted_at, run_id, eval_suite_id, prompt_set_id, scorer_name, score_value, score_value_normalized, normalization_version, sample_n, model_id, model_revision, region (if applicable), route_id (optional), experiment_id (optional), variant_id (optional), attribution_source (router|manual|unknown), and trace_id (optional). Notes: (1) experiment_id/variant_id are omitted (not null) when not available to reduce payload size and simplify downstream cardinality controls; (2) scorer_name is restricted to an allowlist configured in eval-harness to prevent uncontrolled dimensionality.

Integration points: (1) CLI: new flags --experiment-id, --variant-id, --route-id, --attribution-source. (2) Runner API: EvalRunContext gains optional ExperimentAttribution. (3) Publishers: new ScoreStreamPublisher (default off) that can write to stdout (dev), file (integration tests), or the internal bus topic eval-score-stream-v1 (prod). The publisher is behind a config gate (EVAL_SCORE_STREAM_ENABLED) and can be enabled per environment.

Safety/rollout: Backward compatible. Existing artifact formats remain valid; we only add new keys. The score stream publisher is disabled by default. Added guardrails to avoid emitting records when scorer output is missing/NaN. Added sampling controls for very large prompt sets: emit summary scores always; per-prompt scores remain only in artifacts, not in the stream.

Testing: Added unit tests for attribution propagation, JSON schema validation tests for stream records, and an end-to-end fixture that runs a tiny suite and asserts that emitted JSONL contains correct experiment_id/variant_id. Also added a compatibility test to ensure older consumers parsing metadata.json don’t break.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): (1) finalize Console-facing ‘quality’ metric mapping and thresholds (Applied ML + Product); (2) telemetry aggregation jobs (ENG-9027) to roll up score stream by {experiment_id, variant_id}; (3) documentation updates for customers/internal operators on how evals attach to experiments.

Risk notes: Cardinality is controlled by keeping the stream to a small dimension set; do not add raw prompt text or user identifiers. stickiness_key_hash is salted and truncated to prevent correlation across customers while allowing debugging for a single run.
